Passage:
The Mystery of the Terracotta Army
In 1974, Chinese farmers digging a well near the city of Xi'an made an extraordinary discovery. They uncovered pieces of clay that turned out to be parts of life-sized soldiers. This accidental find led archaeologists to one of the greatest archaeological treasures in history: the Terracotta Army.

The Terracotta Army is a collection of thousands of clay soldiers buried underground for over 2,000 years. These statues were created to protect China's first emperor, Qin Shi Huang, in the afterlife. The emperor ruled from 221 to 210 BCE and united China's warring states into one empire. He believed that an army of clay warriors would serve him after death, just as real soldiers served him during his lifetime.

What makes the Terracotta Army truly remarkable is its enormous scale and incredible detail. Archaeologists have found more than 8,000 soldiers so far, along with 130 chariots and 670 horses. Each figure stands about 180 centimetres tall and weighs approximately 200 kilograms. The soldiers are arranged in battle formation, with infantry, cavalry, and archers positioned as they would be in a real army.

Even more impressive is that no two soldiers look exactly alike. Each statue has unique facial features, hairstyles, and expressions. Some soldiers appear young, while others look older and more experienced. Their uniforms indicate different ranks, from generals to foot soldiers. This attention to detail suggests that the sculptors may have modelled the figures on actual members of the emperor's army.

Creating the Terracotta Army required tremendous effort and resources. Historians estimate that approximately 700,000 workers laboured for nearly 40 years to complete the project. The statues were made using local clay, which workers shaped by hand before firing in kilns. Originally, the figures were painted in bright colours including red, green, and purple. However, exposure to air caused most of the paint to fade within minutes of excavation.

The tomb complex covers an area of about 98 square kilometres, making it larger than many modern cities. Despite decades of excavation, archaeologists have only explored a small portion of the site. The emperor's actual burial chamber remains unopened. Ancient texts describe it as containing rivers of mercury and a ceiling decorated with pearls representing stars. Scientists have detected high mercury levels in the soil, suggesting these descriptions might be accurate.

Today, the Terracotta Army attracts millions of visitors annually and is recognised as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. It provides valuable insights into ancient Chinese military organisation, artistic techniques, and burial customs. The discovery continues to reveal new information about one of history's most powerful civilisations and the emperor who shaped China's future.

The Terracotta Army was discovered in 1974 by:
A) archaeologists searching for ancient sites
B) Chinese farmers digging a well
C) historians studying ancient texts
D) tourists visiting Xi'an

Passage:
Title: Why Cursive Writing Deserves a Classroom Comeback
Schools across the country have abandoned cursive writing, claiming keyboards make it obsolete. This decision is short-sighted and damaging. Research demonstrates that learning cursive improves brain development, particularly in areas responsible for memory and fine motor skills. Students who write in cursive show better retention of information compared to those who only print or type. Furthermore, cursive connects us to history—how can future generations read original documents, letters from grandparents, or historical manuscripts if they cannot decipher the script? We're severing a vital link to our past. The solution is simple: reinstate cursive instruction in primary schools. Isn't preserving both cognitive benefits and cultural literacy worth thirty minutes of weekly practice?

Passage:
How Bees Talk Without Words
When a honeybee finds a patch of flowers rich in nectar, it faces an important challenge. How can it tell thousands of other bees in the hive exactly where to fly? Amazingly, bees have developed a special dance language that shares precise directions with their colony.

The most famous bee communication is called the waggle dance. When a worker bee returns to the hive after finding food, it performs this dance on the vertical honeycomb. The bee walks in a figure-eight pattern, waggling its body vigorously in the middle section. Other bees gather around closely, touching the dancer with their antennae to understand the message.

The dance contains two crucial pieces of information: distance and direction. The length of time the bee waggles tells other bees how far they must fly. A short waggle means the flowers are nearby, perhaps only 50 metres away. A longer waggle indicates the food source is farther, possibly up to several kilometres from the hive. Scientists have discovered that bees are remarkably accurate in their distance measurements.

Direction is even more fascinating. Bees use the sun as their compass. If the bee waggles straight up the honeycomb, it means other bees should fly directly toward the sun. If the dance points downward, they should fly away from the sun. An angle to the left or right tells bees to fly at that exact angle relative to the sun's position. This system works even on cloudy days because bees can detect the sun's location through cloud cover.

Bees also communicate through smell and sound. When a dancing bee returns from flowers, it carries the scent of those particular plants on its body. Other bees smell this scent during the dance, helping them recognise the correct flowers when they arrive. The bee also produces buzzing sounds at specific frequencies, which may convey information about the quality of the food source.

This communication system is essential for the survival of the colony. A single hive contains between 20,000 and 80,000 bees, and they must work together efficiently to collect enough nectar and pollen. Without the waggle dance, bees would waste valuable energy searching randomly for food. Instead, they can fly directly to productive locations.

Scientists first decoded the waggle dance in the 1940s, but researchers continue to discover new details about bee communication. Recent studies show that bees can even learn improved dancing techniques from experienced bees, suggesting their language is more sophisticated than previously thought. Understanding how bees share information helps us appreciate these tiny insects and their complex social behaviour. It also reminds us that communication takes many forms in the natural world, not just human speech.

The waggle dance is performed on the:
A) flowers
B) vertical honeycomb
C) ground outside the hive
D) entrance to the hive

Passage:
The Mystery of the Terracotta Army
In 1974, farmers digging a well in China made an incredible discovery. They found pieces of clay pottery that turned out to be part of an enormous underground army. This army was made of terracotta, a type of baked clay, and had been buried for over 2,000 years.

The Terracotta Army was created to protect China's first emperor, Qin Shi Huang, in the afterlife. Emperor Qin ruled from 221 to 210 BCE and united China's warring states into one empire. He believed that he would need protection after death, just as he did during his life. To prepare for this, he ordered the construction of thousands of life-sized clay soldiers.

Archaeologists have uncovered more than 8,000 terracotta figures so far. The army includes soldiers, horses, chariots, and even musicians. Each soldier is unique, with different facial features, hairstyles, and expressions. Some soldiers are kneeling with crossbows, while others are standing ready for battle. The tallest figures are the generals, who can be identified by their elaborate uniforms and headgear.

The creation of this army was an enormous task. Historians estimate that over 700,000 workers spent nearly 40 years building the emperor's tomb complex. Craftsmen used local clay to mould each figure's body parts separately. Arms, legs, and heads were made individually and then attached together. After assembly, the figures were fired in large kilns at high temperatures to make them hard and durable.

Originally, the terracotta soldiers were not grey as they appear today. Scientific tests have revealed traces of bright paint on the figures. The soldiers once wore colourful uniforms in shades of red, blue, green, and purple. Their faces had realistic skin tones, and their hair and beards were black. Unfortunately, when the figures were exposed to air after excavation, the paint quickly dried and flaked off within minutes.

The Terracotta Army was arranged in military formation inside three large pits. The main pit contains about 6,000 soldiers arranged in rows. The second pit holds cavalry units and war chariots. The third pit appears to be the command headquarters, with high-ranking officers. Wooden beams once covered the pits, creating underground chambers, but these beams collapsed over time.

Today, the site is a museum that attracts millions of visitors each year. Archaeologists continue to excavate carefully, using modern technology to preserve the figures better. They now spray special chemicals on newly uncovered soldiers to protect any remaining paint. Many parts of the tomb complex remain unexcavated, and experts believe more discoveries await beneath the ground. The Terracotta Army stands as a remarkable example of ancient Chinese art, engineering, and beliefs about life after death.

The Terracotta Army was discovered in 1974 by:
A) archaeologists
B) farmers
C) historians
D) museum workers
E) soldiers

Passage:
Title: Ink That Vanishes: Messages in Lemon Juice
Long before encrypted emails, people concealed messages using invisible ink made from everyday substances. Lemon juice was particularly popular during wartime because it leaves no visible trace when applied to paper. The acidic liquid weakens the paper's fibres, but this damage only becomes apparent when heat is applied. Holding the paper near a candle flame causes the weakened areas to brown faster than the surrounding paper, revealing the hidden text. This simple chemistry allowed resistance fighters and spies to pass information under the noses of their enemies. The technique required no special equipment—just fruit, a pen nib, and a heat source—making it accessible yet remarkably effective for clandestine communication.

What made lemon juice invisible ink particularly useful for wartime spies?

A) It was the only substance that could create invisible messages
B) It was expensive and therefore trusted by military officials
C) It required no specialized tools and left no suspicious traces
D) It could only be revealed by trained intelligence officers
E) It was faster to use than other forms of secret communication

Title: The Clockmaker's Final Masterpiece
The workshop had fallen silent three decades ago, yet dust motes still danced through shafts of amber light filtering between warped wooden shutters. Hundreds of timepieces lined the walls—grandfather clocks with painted moons, pocket watches dangling from rusty hooks, cuckoo clocks with doors frozen half-open. In the centre stood an enormous brass mechanism, taller than a man, its gears interlocking like metallic lace. The Ancient Incan Road System: Connecting an Empire
High in the mountains of South America, an incredible network of roads once connected a vast empire. The Inca civilisation built over 40,000 kilometres of roads across some of the world's most challenging terrain. This remarkable achievement linked their empire from modern-day Colombia to Chile, crossing deserts, mountains, and rainforests.

The Incan road system, called Qhapaq Ñan, was constructed without the use of wheels or iron tools. Workers used stone hammers and bronze chisels to carve paths through solid rock. In flat areas, roads were up to 15 metres wide. In mountainous regions, they became narrow stone staircases climbing steep slopes. Some sections even included rope bridges that swung across deep valleys.

These roads served many important purposes for the empire. Messengers called chasquis ran along the routes, carrying news and information between cities. They could travel up to 240 kilometres in a single day by passing messages to the next runner at relay stations. The roads also allowed armies to move quickly across the empire, helping the Inca maintain control over distant regions. Additionally, traders used the routes to transport goods like food, textiles, and precious metals.

The Inca built their roads with careful planning and impressive engineering skills. In swampy areas, workers created raised causeways to keep the road surface dry. Through deserts, they placed marker posts to guide travellers when sand covered the path. In the mountains, they constructed drainage systems to prevent rainwater from washing away the roads. Stone retaining walls supported roads built on steep hillsides.

Rest houses called tambos were positioned every 20 to 30 kilometres along major routes. These buildings provided shelter, food, and supplies for official travellers. Storage facilities at tambos held emergency food reserves for times of famine. Some tambos were simple structures, whilst others were elaborate complexes with multiple buildings.

The road system reflected the Inca's strong central government and organised society. Only people on official business received permission to use the main roads. Common citizens needed special approval to travel. This control helped the empire manage resources and maintain security.

Passage: The Secret Language of Bees

When a honeybee discovers a patch of flowers rich with nectar, it faces an important challenge. How can it tell thousands of other bees in the hive exactly where to find this food? The answer lies in one of nature's most fascinating communication systems: the waggle dance.

Karl von Frisch, an Austrian scientist, first decoded this bee language in the 1940s. He observed that when a forager bee returns to the hive, it performs a special figure-eight dance on the honeycomb. Other bees gather around closely, touching the dancer with their antennae to understand the message. This discovery was so significant that von Frisch won the Nobel Prize in 1973.

The waggle dance contains precise information about distance and direction. When a bee waggles its body in a straight line, the angle of this line indicates direction. If the bee dances straight up the honeycomb, the flowers are in the direction of the sun. If the bee dances at an angle to the right, other bees must fly at that same angle from the sun's position. The sun acts like a compass in the sky.

Distance information is equally clever. The length of the waggle run tells other bees how far they must travel. A short waggle means the flowers are nearby, perhaps 50 metres away. A longer, more vigorous waggle indicates a journey of several kilometres. The bee also communicates the quality of the food source through the enthusiasm of its dance. Better nectar sources inspire more energetic performances.

Bees can even adjust their dance for the sun's movement across the sky. Since the sun changes position throughout the day, bees update their directional instructions accordingly. This shows remarkable mental ability for such a small creature. A bee's brain is smaller than a grain of rice, yet it can perform complex calculations.

This communication system benefits the entire colony. Instead of thousands of bees searching randomly, they work as an efficient team. Bees can share information about flowers up to six kilometres away. This organised approach helps the hive collect enough nectar to produce honey and feed the colony, including the queen and developing larvae.

Scientists continue to study bee communication today. Recent research shows that bees can also communicate about water sources and new nesting sites. Some studies suggest that bees might even have regional dialects, with different populations using slightly different dance patterns. Understanding how bees share information helps us appreciate their intelligence and reminds us why protecting bee populations matters. These tiny dancers play a vital role in pollinating crops and maintaining healthy ecosystems around the world.

Karl von Frisch won the Nobel Prize in:
A) 1940
B) 1973
C) 1928
D) 1950

Building Bridges Without Nails
In ancient China and Japan, carpenters developed a remarkable way to build wooden structures without using a single nail or screw. This technique, called joinery, relies on precisely cut pieces of wood that fit together like a three-dimensional puzzle. Each piece locks into place through clever shapes such as dovetails, mortises and tenons. These joints are so strong that many wooden temples and bridges have stood for over a thousand years, surviving earthquakes and storms. Modern architects still study these traditional methods because the flexible joints allow buildings to move slightly during earth tremors, actually making them safer than rigid structures.

Passage:
How Cities Keep Cool: The Urban Heat Island Effect
On a hot summer night, city dwellers often notice something unusual. The temperature in the city centre feels much warmer than in nearby parks or countryside. This difference is not imaginary. It is caused by what scientists call the urban heat island effect.

An urban heat island forms when a city becomes significantly warmer than the rural areas surrounding it. The temperature difference can be quite dramatic. Some cities are up to seven degrees Celsius hotter than their surroundings. This extra heat affects millions of people living in urban areas around the world.

Several factors create this heat island effect. Dark surfaces like roads and rooftops absorb sunlight during the day. These surfaces then release the stored heat at night. Concrete and asphalt hold much more heat than soil or grass. Buildings also trap heat by blocking wind that would normally cool an area. Additionally, air conditioners and vehicles release warm air into city streets. All these elements combine to make cities noticeably warmer.

The consequences of urban heat islands impact daily life in many ways. Higher temperatures mean people use more electricity to cool their homes and offices. This increased energy use costs more money and creates more pollution. Hot cities also affect human health. Elderly people and young children struggle more in extreme heat. Some people develop heat-related illnesses during summer heatwaves. The extra warmth can even change local weather patterns, sometimes causing more thunderstorms.

Fortunately, cities can take action to reduce these heat islands. Planting trees provides natural shade and cooling. Trees release water vapour through their leaves, which helps lower air temperature. Some cities paint rooftops white or install special reflective materials. These light-coloured surfaces bounce sunlight away instead of absorbing it. Creating more parks and green spaces also helps. Grass and plants stay much cooler than concrete.

Singapore offers an excellent example of heat island solutions. The city has planted trees along most streets and created rooftop gardens on buildings. Melbourne has increased its tree canopy and built more parks. Los Angeles has started painting some streets with light-grey coating. These projects show promising results.

Understanding urban heat islands matters now more than ever. The world's population continues to grow, with more people moving to cities each year. Climate change is also making summers hotter in many regions. By learning about heat islands and their solutions, communities can create cooler, healthier, and more comfortable urban environments. Simple changes like planting trees and using lighter building materials can make a real difference. Cities do not have to be heat traps. With careful planning and smart design, urban areas can stay cooler for everyone.

Los Angeles has started painting some streets with:
A) white coating
B) light-grey coating
C) reflective materials
D) dark coating

The Language of Drums in West Africa
For centuries, communities across West Africa have used talking drums to communicate across vast distances. These hourglass-shaped instruments can mimic the tonal patterns of spoken language, allowing drummers to send complex messages between villages. During important events like festivals or emergencies, skilled drummers translate speech into rhythmic sequences that can be heard up to eight kilometres away. The talking drum serves not merely as a musical instrument but as a sophisticated communication system that predates modern technology. Today, whilst mobile phones have largely replaced this method, many communities still preserve the art form, teaching young people the intricate rhythms that once connected their ancestors across the landscape.

Passage: Why Schools Must Ban Homework for Primary Students

Every evening, millions of children around the world sit at kitchen tables, struggling through homework instead of playing, exploring or simply being children. It's time we asked ourselves a crucial question: does homework for primary school students actually help them learn, or is it doing more harm than good?

The evidence is clear. Research from leading universities shows that homework provides no academic benefit for primary-aged children. A comprehensive study by Duke University found that homework in primary school has zero correlation with academic achievement. Zero. Yet we continue to burden young students with worksheets and assignments that steal precious hours from their childhood. Why do we persist with a practice that science tells us doesn't work?

Consider what children lose when homework dominates their evenings. They lose time for creative play, which experts agree is essential for brain development. They lose opportunities for physical activity, contributing to rising childhood obesity rates. They lose quality time with family, something that research consistently links to better mental health and stronger social skills. Are we really willing to sacrifice all of this for a practice that delivers no educational benefits?

Furthermore, homework creates unnecessary stress and anxiety in young children. Paediatricians report increasing numbers of primary students experiencing headaches, stomach aches and sleep problems directly related to homework pressure. Some children as young as seven years old cry over assignments they don't understand, whilst their exhausted parents struggle to help after their own long working days. This isn't education; it's cruelty.

Some argue that homework teaches responsibility and time management. But children learn these skills through age-appropriate activities like caring for pets, helping with household tasks, and managing their own belongings. They don't need worksheets to develop life skills.

Countries like Finland, which consistently ranks among the world's best education systems, assign minimal homework in primary years. Passage:
Title: Echoes in Stone: How Acoustics Shaped Ancient Theatres
The architects of ancient Greece possessed an extraordinary understanding of acoustics, engineering amphitheatres where a whisper on stage could reach audiences seated seventy metres away. At Epidaurus, constructed in the fourth century BCE, limestone seats were meticulously arranged in a precise geometric pattern that filtered out low-frequency background noise whilst amplifying the actors' voices. Modern researchers initially attributed this phenomenon to the theatre's semicircular shape, but recent studies reveal that the calcite-rich stone itself acts as an acoustic filter, suppressing ambient sound frequencies below five hundred hertz. This sophisticated manipulation of natural materials demonstrates that ancient civilisations employed scientific principles centuries before formal acoustical theory emerged.

Passage:
Why Every Student Deserves a Later School Start Time
Every morning, millions of teenagers around the world drag themselves out of bed in the dark, fighting against their own biology to make it to school on time. This daily struggle is not laziness—it is science. Schools must start later to protect the health and success of their students.

The evidence is overwhelming. Research from sleep scientists shows that teenage brains are wired differently from adult brains. During adolescence, our internal body clocks shift forward by up to two hours. This means that asking a thirteen-year-old to fall asleep at nine o'clock is like asking an adult to sleep at seven o'clock. It simply does not work. Yet schools continue to ring the bell at eight-thirty or even earlier, forcing students to wake up when their bodies are still producing sleep hormones.

What are the consequences of this mismatch? Students arrive at school exhausted, unable to concentrate or learn effectively. Studies across the United Kingdom, United States and Australia reveal that sleep-deprived teenagers perform significantly worse in mathematics and reading. Their grades drop, their creativity suffers, and their memory weakens. Is this really the best we can offer our young people?

The health impacts are even more alarming. Chronic sleep loss in teenagers increases rates of depression, anxiety and obesity. It weakens immune systems, making students sick more often. Tired teenagers are also involved in more car accidents and injuries. These are not minor inconveniences—they are serious threats to wellbeing.

Some critics argue that later start times would disrupt family schedules or sporting activities. However, dozens of schools that have made the change report positive results across the board. In Seattle, when high schools shifted their start time from seven-fifty to eight-forty-five, students gained an average of thirty-four minutes of sleep per night. Attendance improved, grades rose, and tardiness decreased. Teachers reported more alert, engaged students. The benefits clearly outweigh the temporary adjustment challenges.

Others claim that teenagers should simply go to bed earlier. This argument ignores biological reality. We cannot command our bodies to sleep any more than we can command ourselves to grow taller. Teenagers are not choosing to stay awake—their brains are following a natural developmental pattern.

Every student deserves the opportunity to learn at their best. We provide nutritious meals, safe buildings and qualified teachers. Why would we not also provide appropriate schedules that match how young bodies actually work? The research is clear, the solutions are proven, and the stakes are high. It is time for schools to put student health first and move their clocks forward. Our teenagers are worth it.

A) Teenagers need more hours of sleep than adults
B) Teenage body clocks shift forward by up to two hours
C) Teenagers produce more sleep hormones
D) Teenagers are less able to wake up early

Passage:
Why Our Neighbourhood Parks Are Worth Fighting For
Every week, another green space disappears beneath concrete and steel. Developers promise progress, but what they deliver is the destruction of our community's lungs. We must act now to protect our neighbourhood parks before they vanish forever.

Parks are not luxuries—they are necessities. Research from the University of Melbourne shows that children who play in green spaces for at least one hour daily perform 20% better academically and demonstrate significantly lower stress levels. These aren't just statistics; they represent real students achieving their potential because they had somewhere to run, climb, and breathe fresh air.

Yet developers continue to target our parks. They claim that new apartment buildings will solve housing shortages, but at what cost? When Marrickville Council sold Patterson Park to developers in 2019, local doctors reported a 35% increase in childhood obesity within two years. The park had been the community's free gymnasium, its outdoor classroom, its meeting place. Now it's a carpark.

Some argue that we need housing more than parks. This argument presents a false choice. Cities like Singapore have proven that we can build upwards whilst preserving green spaces. Their Housing Development Board mandates that every neighbourhood must have parkland within 400 metres of residential buildings. Why can't Australian councils adopt similar policies?

Consider the economic benefits too. Properties near parks sell for 15% more than identical homes without park access. Parks increase property values for entire neighbourhoods, generating higher council rates that fund community services. Destroying parks for short-term development gains is economically foolish.

Moretheless, the environmental argument remains most compelling. Urban parks reduce air pollution, lower temperatures during heatwaves, and provide crucial habitats for native wildlife. As climate change intensifies, these green spaces become even more vital. Removing them is like throwing away a life jacket whilst the waters rise around us.

What can we do? First, attend council meetings and voice opposition to park developments. Second, sign petitions and encourage neighbours to do likewise. Third, support councillors who prioritise green space protection. Our voices matter—when Leichhardt residents protested the proposed sale of Pioneer Park, the council reversed its decision within three weeks.

Parks belong to everyone. They don't discriminate based on wealth, age, or background. A child in public housing enjoys the same swings as a child from a mansion. This equality is precious and rare.

We stand at a crossroads. We can allow developers to pave over our green spaces, or we can fight for our children's right to nature. The choice seems obvious. Our parks are worth fighting for—because once they're gone, they're gone forever. Will you join the fight?

D) Most residents did not support the park's protection
E) The protest was unsuccessful in saving the park

Passage:
Why Every Student Deserves a Musical Education
When school budgets are tight, music programmes are often the first to face the axe. This is a grave mistake that threatens the future of our children and our society.

Music education is not a luxury—it is a necessity. Research from universities worldwide consistently demonstrates that students who participate in music programmes perform better academically across all subjects. A landmark study involving over 100,000 students found that those who learnt musical instruments scored an average of 20% higher in mathematics and science tests. Why? Because music trains the brain to recognise patterns, maintain focus, and solve complex problems. These are precisely the skills that employers desperately seek in today's workforce.

Yet some administrators argue that music is merely entertainment, a pleasant distraction from 'real' learning. How short-sighted can we be? Would we dismiss mathematics because not every student becomes an engineer? Of course not. Music develops critical thinking, discipline, and creativity—qualities essential for success in any career path.

Consider the economic argument. Critics claim music programmes cost too much, but what price do we place on a child's potential? The instruments, teachers, and facilities required for music education cost less than many sports programmes, yet music receives a fraction of the funding. Schools routinely spend thousands on football equipment, but baulk at purchasing violins or hiring qualified music teachers. This reveals a troubling bias in our priorities.

Moreover, music education promotes mental health and emotional wellbeing. In an era where teenage anxiety and depression have reached alarming levels, music provides a vital outlet for self-expression and stress relief. Students who play instruments or sing in choirs report higher levels of confidence and stronger social connections. They learn teamwork through orchestras and bands, discovering how individual contributions create something greater than themselves.

The cultural benefits cannot be ignored either. Music connects us to our heritage and helps us understand diverse cultures. When students learn folk songs from different countries or explore various musical traditions, they develop empathy and global awareness. In our increasingly interconnected world, these qualities are invaluable.

Some argue that students can learn music outside school through private lessons. But this creates inequality. Only wealthy families can afford private tuition, meaning that talented children from disadvantaged backgrounds miss opportunities to develop their gifts. School music programmes level the playing field, ensuring every child—regardless of family income—can access this transformative education.

The evidence is overwhelming and the moral case is clear. We must prioritise music education in our schools, not as an optional extra, but as a fundamental component of every student's development. Our children deserve nothing less than a complete education—one that nurtures their minds, hearts, and souls. Will we give it to them?

A) Music teachers are too expensive to employ
B) Schools consider music less important than other subjects
C) Music instruments require too much maintenance
D) Students prefer sports programmes over music
E) Music education does not improve academic performance

When Ink Met Paper: Ancient China's Printing Revolution
Long before computers and photocopiers, Chinese inventors revolutionised communication through woodblock printing during the Tang Dynasty, around 868 CE. Artisans carved entire pages of text backwards into wooden blocks, coated them with ink, and pressed paper against them to create copies. This innovation laid the groundwork for mass communication centuries before Gutenberg's European printing press.

Passage:
Why Every Student Deserves a Later School Start Time
Every morning, millions of teenagers around the world drag themselves out of bed in the dark, fighting against their own biology to make it to school on time. This daily struggle is not laziness—it is science. Schools must start later to protect the health and success of their students.

The evidence is overwhelming. Research from sleep scientists shows that teenage brains are wired differently from adult brains. During adolescence, our internal body clocks shift forward by up to two hours. This means that asking a thirteen-year-old to fall asleep at nine o'clock is like asking an adult to sleep at seven o'clock. It simply does not work. Yet schools continue to ring the bell at eight-thirty or even earlier, forcing students to wake up when their bodies are still producing sleep hormones.

What are the consequences of this mismatch? Students arrive at school exhausted, unable to concentrate or learn effectively. Studies across the United Kingdom, United States and Australia reveal that sleep-deprived teenagers perform significantly worse in mathematics and reading. Their grades drop, their creativity suffers, and their memory weakens. Is this really the best we can offer our young people?

The health impacts are even more alarming. Chronic sleep loss in teenagers increases rates of depression, anxiety and obesity. It weakens immune systems, making students sick more often. Tired teenagers are also involved in more car accidents and injuries. These are not minor inconveniences—they are serious threats to wellbeing.

Some critics argue that later start times would disrupt family schedules or sporting activities. However, dozens of schools that have made the change report positive results across the board. In Seattle, when high schools shifted their start time from seven-fifty to eight-forty-five, students gained an average of thirty-four minutes of sleep per night. Attendance improved, grades rose, and tardiness decreased. Teachers reported more alert, engaged students. The benefits clearly outweigh the temporary adjustment challenges.

Others claim that teenagers should simply go to bed earlier. This argument ignores biological reality. We cannot command our bodies to sleep any more than we can command ourselves to grow taller. Teenagers are not choosing to stay awake—their brains are following a natural developmental pattern.

Every student deserves the opportunity to learn at their best. We provide nutritious meals, safe buildings and qualified teachers. Why would we not also provide appropriate schedules that match how young bodies actually work? The research is clear, the solutions are proven, and the stakes are high. It is time for schools to put student health first and move their clocks forward. Our teenagers are worth it.

A) The author believes they have not considered the scientific evidence

Passage: Why School Should Start Later: A Wake-Up Call for Better Learning

Every morning across our nation, millions of teenagers drag themselves out of bed in the darkness, fighting exhaustion as they prepare for another early school day. This daily struggle isn't just about being tired—it's about a system that ignores basic biology and damages our young people's health, learning, and future.

The science is crystal clear. Adolescent bodies naturally fall asleep later and need more rest than adults. During the teenage years, our internal body clocks shift, making it nearly impossible to fall asleep before 11 pm. Yet most secondary schools start at 8:30 am or earlier, forcing students to wake at 6 am or before. This creates a serious sleep deficit that accumulates throughout the week.

What are the consequences of this exhaustion? Research from the Australian Sleep Health Foundation shows that sleep-deprived teenagers perform significantly worse in tests, struggle with concentration, and have higher rates of anxiety and depression. A landmark American study involving over 9,000 students found that schools starting after 8:30 am saw dramatic improvements: better grades, fewer absences, and reduced car accidents among student drivers. These aren't minor benefits—they're life-changing outcomes.

Some argue that later start times would disrupt family schedules and after-school activities. But isn't our children's health worth some adjustment? Parents already rearrange their lives for countless school requirements. Sports practices can be rescheduled. The alternative—continuing to harm our teenagers' development—is simply unacceptable.

A) Because family schedules are not important
B) Because parents already make adjustments for school requirements
C) Because after-school activities should be cancelled
D) Because teenagers can manage their own schedules

Passage: The Ancient City Beneath the Waves: Discovering Thonis-Heracleion

For over a thousand years, an entire city lay hidden beneath the Mediterranean Sea. This lost city, called Thonis-Heracleion, was once a bustling port in ancient Egypt. Today, archaeologists are uncovering its secrets and learning how people lived more than 2,000 years ago.

Thonis-Heracleion was located near the mouth of the Nile River. It served as Egypt's main gateway for trade with Greece and other Mediterranean countries. Ships carrying grain, papyrus, and precious stones would dock at its busy harbour. The city was home to grand temples, towering statues, and wealthy merchants. Ancient Greek historians even wrote about visiting this important trading centre.

Sometime around the eighth century CE, the city disappeared. Scientists believe several natural disasters caused Thonis-Heracleion to sink into the sea. The soft clay and sand beneath the city became unstable. Earthquakes shook the ground, making buildings collapse. Tidal waves crashed over the streets. Slowly, the entire city slipped underwater and was covered by sand and mud.

For centuries, people forgot about Thonis-Heracleion. Some historians thought it was just a myth, a story that ancient writers had invented. However, in 2000, French archaeologist Franck Goddio and his team made an extraordinary discovery. Using special underwater equipment, they found the ruins of the lost city in Aboukir Bay, about 6.5 kilometres off Egypt's northern coast.

The underwater city is remarkably well preserved. Divers have found massive stone statues of Egyptian gods and pharaohs, some standing over five metres tall. They have recovered thousands of gold coins, jewellery, and bronze objects. Ancient ships still rest on the seabed, their wooden hulls intact after centuries underwater. Stone anchors and pottery jars reveal details about ancient trade routes and daily life.

One of the most significant discoveries was the remains of a huge temple dedicated to the god Amun-Gereb. Archaeologists found enormous blocks of stone, columns, and religious objects scattered across the seafloor. These findings help researchers understand ancient Egyptian religious practices and beliefs.

Exploring Thonis-Heracleion is challenging work. Divers can only stay underwater for limited periods. The water is murky, making visibility poor. Moving heavy stone objects from the seabed requires careful planning and special equipment. Despite these difficulties, archaeologists continue their work, bringing up new treasures each year.

The discovery of Thonis-Heracleion reminds us that our planet still holds many secrets. It shows how natural forces can dramatically change landscapes and hide entire civilisations. By studying this sunken city, we gain valuable knowledge about ancient trade, religion, and architecture. Each object brought to the surface adds another piece to the puzzle of human history, helping us understand how our ancestors lived, worked, and built their remarkable world.

The huge temple found underwater was dedicated to:
A) Egyptian pharaohs
B) Amun-Gereb
C) Greek gods
D) Mediterranean traders

Passage:
Title: Bioluminescence: Nature's Living Light
Deep within the ocean's aphotic zone, where sunlight cannot penetrate, countless organisms generate their own luminescence through a chemical reaction called bioluminescence. This extraordinary phenomenon occurs when luciferin molecules interact with oxygen, catalysed by the enzyme luciferase, producing light without heat. While fireflies employ this mechanism to attract mates in terrestrial environments, marine creatures have evolved far more diverse applications: anglerfish dangle glowing lures to ensnare prey, whilst certain squid species deploy bioluminescent ink as a diversionary tactic against predators. Scientists estimate that approximately ninety per cent of deep-sea organisms possess this remarkable capability, transforming the abyssal darkness into a constellation of living stars.

Title: Weaving Water: The Ancient Qanat System
For over 3000 years, communities across Persia and Central Asia have relied on qanats—underground channels that transport water from mountain aquifers to arid lowlands. These remarkable tunnels, some stretching over 70 kilometres, were dug entirely by hand using precise calculations to maintain a gentle downward slope. Vertical shafts, spaced at regular intervals along the route, allowed workers to excavate the channel and provided ventilation. The qanat's underground design prevents evaporation in hot climates, delivering fresh water year-round without pumps or modern technology. Today, approximately 37,000 qanats still operate across Iran, sustaining agriculture and providing drinking water to millions of people in desert regions.

The soft clay and sand beneath the city became unstable. Earthquakes shook the ground, making buildings collapse. Tidal waves crashed over the streets. Slowly, the entire city slipped underwater and was covered by sand and mud.

For centuries, people forgot about Thonis-Heracleion. Some historians thought it was just a myth, a story that ancient writers had invented. However, in 2000, French archaeologist Franck Goddio and his team made an extraordinary discovery. Using special underwater equipment, they found the ruins of the lost city in Aboukir Bay, about 6.5 kilometres off Egypt's northern coast.

The underwater city is remarkably well preserved. Divers have found massive stone statues of Egyptian gods and pharaohs, some standing over five metres tall. They have recovered thousands of gold coins, jewellery, and bronze objects. Ancient ships still rest on the seabed, their wooden hulls intact after centuries underwater. Stone anchors and pottery jars reveal details about ancient trade routes and daily life.

One of the most significant discoveries was the remains of a huge temple dedicated to the god Amun-Gereb. Archaeologists found enormous blocks of stone, columns, and religious objects scattered across the seafloor. These findings help researchers understand ancient Egyptian religious practices and beliefs.

Exploring Thonis-Heracleion is challenging work. Divers can only stay underwater for limited periods. The water is murky, making visibility poor. Moving heavy stone objects from the seabed requires careful planning and special equipment. Despite these difficulties, archaeologists continue their work, bringing up new treasures each year.

The discovery of Thonis-Heracleion reminds us that our planet still holds many secrets. It shows how natural forces can dramatically change landscapes and hide entire civilisations. By studying this sunken city, we gain valuable knowledge about ancient trade, religion, and architecture. Each object brought to the surface adds another piece to the puzzle of human history, helping us understand how our ancestors lived, worked, and built their remarkable world.

Passage: Why Schools Must Ban Homework for Primary Students

Every evening, millions of children around the world sit at kitchen tables, struggling through homework instead of playing, exploring or simply being children. It's time we asked ourselves a crucial question: does homework for primary school students actually help them learn, or is it doing more harm than good?

The evidence is clear. Research from leading universities shows that homework provides no academic benefit for primary-aged children. A comprehensive study by Duke University found that homework in primary school has zero correlation with academic achievement. Zero. Yet we continue to burden young students with worksheets and assignments that steal precious hours from their childhood. Why do we persist with a practice that science tells us doesn't work?

Consider what children lose when homework dominates their evenings. They lose time for creative play, which experts agree is essential for brain development. They lose opportunities for physical activity, contributing to rising childhood obesity rates. They lose quality time with family, something that research consistently links to better mental health and stronger social skills. Are we really willing to sacrifice all of this for a practice that delivers no educational benefits?

Furthermore, homework creates unnecessary stress and anxiety in young children. Paediatricians report increasing numbers of primary students experiencing headaches, stomach aches and sleep problems directly related to homework pressure. Some children as young as seven years old cry over assignments they don't understand, whilst their exhausted parents struggle to help after their own long working days. This isn't education; it's cruelty.

Some argue that homework teaches responsibility and time management. But children learn these skills through age-appropriate activities like caring for pets, helping with household tasks, and managing their own belongings. They don't need worksheets to develop life skills.

Countries like Finland, which consistently ranks among the world's best education systems, assign minimal homework in primary years. Their students spend afternoons playing outdoors, pursuing hobbies and spending time with family. They arrive at school refreshed, curious and eager to learn. Meanwhile, in countries drowning students in homework, we see increased anxiety, decreased motivation and no improvement in actual learning outcomes.

The solution is simple and supported by evidence: ban homework for all primary school students. Let children be children. Let them play, explore, rest and grow. Let families enjoy their evenings together without the constant shadow of unfinished assignments. Our children deserve better than a system that wastes their time and damages their wellbeing for zero educational gain. It's time for schools to listen to the research, show courage, and make this essential change.

A) They dislike school because they have no homework
B) They are more motivated to learn than students with heavy homework
C) They perform poorly due to lack of homework practice
D) They spend too much time playing outdoors
E) They need more homework to develop responsibility

Passage:
Title: Competing Visions for Medieval Cathedrals
In twelfth-century Europe, two distinct architectural styles competed for dominance in cathedral design. The Romanesque approach favoured thick stone walls, small windows, and rounded arches that created dark, fortress-like interiors. These structures projected strength and permanence. In contrast, the emerging Gothic style employed pointed arches, flying buttresses, and vast stained-glass windows. This innovation allowed walls to soar higher whilst flooding interiors with coloured light. Where Romanesque builders prioritised solidity and defence, Gothic architects pursued height and luminosity. Both styles reflected their creators' values: one emphasising earthly protection, the other reaching towards the heavens. The shift between them marked a profound change in how Europeans conceived sacred space.

Passage:
How Cities Keep Cool: The Urban Heat Island Effect
On a hot summer night, city dwellers often notice something unusual. The temperature in the city centre feels much warmer than in nearby parks or countryside. This difference is not imaginary. It is caused by what scientists call the urban heat island effect.

An urban heat island forms when a city becomes significantly warmer than the rural areas surrounding it. The temperature difference can be quite dramatic. Some cities are up to seven degrees Celsius hotter than their surroundings. This extra heat affects millions of people living in urban areas around the world.

Several factors create this heat island effect. Dark surfaces like roads and rooftops absorb sunlight during the day. These surfaces then release the stored heat at night. Concrete and asphalt hold much more heat than soil or grass. Buildings also trap heat by blocking wind that would normally cool an area. Additionally, air conditioners and vehicles release warm air into city streets. All these elements combine to make cities noticeably warmer.

The consequences of urban heat islands impact daily life in many ways. Higher temperatures mean people use more electricity to cool their homes and offices. This increased energy use costs more money and creates more pollution. Hot cities also affect human health. Elderly people and young children struggle more in extreme heat. Some people develop heat-related illnesses during summer heatwaves. The extra warmth can even change local weather patterns, sometimes causing more thunderstorms.

Fortunately, cities can take action to reduce these heat islands. Planting trees provides natural shade and cooling. Trees release water vapour through their leaves, which helps lower air temperature. Some cities paint rooftops white or install special reflective materials. These light-coloured surfaces bounce sunlight away instead of absorbing it. Creating more parks and green spaces also helps. Grass and plants stay much cooler than concrete.

Singapore offers an excellent example of heat island solutions. The city has planted trees along most streets and created rooftop gardens on buildings. Melbourne has increased its tree canopy and built more parks. Los Angeles has started painting some streets with light-grey coating. These projects show promising results.

Understanding urban heat islands matters now more than ever. The world's population continues to grow, with more people moving to cities each year. Climate change is also making summers hotter in many regions. By learning about heat islands and their solutions, communities can create cooler, healthier, and more comfortable urban environments. Simple changes like planting trees and using lighter building materials can make a real difference. Cities do not have to be heat traps. With careful planning and smart design, urban areas can stay cooler for everyone.

A) Three degrees Celsius
B) Five degrees Celsius
C) Seven degrees Celsius
D) Ten degrees Celsius
E) Twelve degrees Celsius

Passage:
Why Every Student Deserves a Later School Start Time
Every morning, millions of teenagers around the world drag themselves out of bed in the dark, fighting against their own biology to make it to school on time. This daily struggle is not laziness—it is science. Schools must start later to protect the health and success of their students.

The evidence is overwhelming. Research from sleep scientists shows that teenage brains are wired differently from adult brains. During adolescence, our internal body clocks shift forward by up to two hours. This means that asking a thirteen-year-old to fall asleep at nine o'clock is like asking an adult to sleep at seven o'clock. It simply does not work. Yet schools continue to ring the bell at eight-thirty or even earlier, forcing students to wake up when their bodies are still producing sleep hormones.

What are the consequences of this mismatch? Students arrive at school exhausted, unable to concentrate or learn effectively. Studies across the United Kingdom, United States and Australia reveal that sleep-deprived teenagers perform significantly worse in mathematics and reading. Their grades drop, their creativity suffers, and their memory weakens. Is this really the best we can offer our young people?

The health impacts are even more alarming. Chronic sleep loss in teenagers increases rates of depression, anxiety and obesity. It weakens immune systems, making students sick more often. Tired teenagers are also involved in more car accidents and injuries. These are not minor inconveniences—they are serious threats to wellbeing.

Some critics argue that later start times would disrupt family schedules or sporting activities. However, dozens of schools that have made the change report positive results across the board. In Seattle, when high schools shifted their start time from seven-fifty to eight-forty-five, students gained an average of thirty-four minutes of sleep per night. Attendance improved, grades rose, and tardiness decreased. Teachers reported more alert, engaged students. The benefits clearly outweigh the temporary adjustment challenges.

Others claim that teenagers should simply go to bed earlier. This argument ignores biological reality. We cannot command our bodies to sleep any more than we can command ourselves to grow taller. Teenagers are not choosing to stay awake—their brains are following a natural developmental pattern.

Every student deserves the opportunity to learn at their best. We provide nutritious meals, safe buildings and qualified teachers. Why would we not also provide appropriate schedules that match how young bodies actually work? The research is clear, the solutions are proven, and the stakes are high. It is time for schools to put student health first and move their clocks forward. Our teenagers are worth it.

A) To show that both processes happen during adolescence
B) To explain that sleep patterns cannot be controlled by willpower alone
C) To prove that teenagers need more sleep than adults
D) To suggest that sleep is important for physical growth

Passage: The Secret Language of Bees

When a honeybee discovers a patch of flowers rich with nectar, it faces an important challenge. How can it tell thousands of other bees in the hive exactly where to find this food? The answer lies in one of nature's most fascinating communication systems: the waggle dance.

Karl von Frisch, an Austrian scientist, first decoded this bee language in the 1940s. He observed that when a forager bee returns to the hive, it performs a special figure-eight dance on the honeycomb. Other bees gather around closely, touching the dancer with their antennae to understand the message. This discovery was so significant that von Frisch won the Nobel Prize in 1973.

The waggle dance contains precise information about distance and direction. When a bee waggles its body in a straight line, the angle of this line indicates direction. If the bee dances straight up the honeycomb, the flowers are in the direction of the sun. If the bee dances at an angle to the right, other bees must fly at that same angle from the sun's position. The sun acts like a compass in the sky.

Distance information is equally clever. The length of the waggle run tells other bees how far they must travel. A short waggle means the flowers are nearby, perhaps 50 metres away. A longer, more vigorous waggle indicates a journey of several kilometres. The bee also communicates the quality of the food source through the enthusiasm of its dance. Better nectar sources inspire more energetic performances.

Bees can even adjust their dance for the sun's movement across the sky. Since the sun changes position throughout the day, bees update their directional instructions accordingly. This shows remarkable mental ability for such a small creature. A bee's brain is smaller than a grain of rice, yet it can perform complex calculations.

This communication system benefits the entire colony. Instead of thousands of bees searching randomly, they work as an efficient team. Bees can share information about flowers up to six kilometres away. This organised approach helps the hive collect enough nectar to produce honey and feed the colony, including the queen and developing larvae.

Scientists continue to study bee communication today. Recent research shows that bees can also communicate about water sources and new nesting sites. Some studies suggest that bees might even have regional dialects, with different populations using slightly different dance patterns. Understanding how bees share information helps us appreciate their intelligence and reminds us why protecting bee populations matters. These tiny dancers play a vital role in pollinating crops and maintaining healthy ecosystems around the world.

Karl von Frisch decoded the bee language in the:
A) 1930s
B) 1940s
C) 1950s
D) 1970s

Passage:
The Colour of Courage
Amara pressed her nose against the cold window of the train, watching the English countryside blur into shades of green and grey. Three months ago, she had been in Lagos, surrounded by the bright colours and warm voices of home. Now, she was heading to Millbrook Grammar School, where she would be the only Nigerian girl in Year Seven.

"You'll do brilliantly," her mother had said at the station that morning, straightening Amara's new uniform. But her mother's eyes had looked worried, and that worry now sat heavy in Amara's stomach.

The school hall buzzed with excited chatter as students reunited after the summer holidays. Amara stood near the door, clutching her timetable. Groups of girls laughed together, their ponytails swinging as they hugged. Nobody looked her way.

"Lost?" A voice startled her. A red-haired girl with freckles smiled kindly. "I'm Sophie. You're new, right?"

Amara nodded, relief flooding through her. "I'm Amara. I just moved from Nigeria."

"Brilliant! Come sit with us at lunch."

But at lunch, everything changed. Sophie's friends, Maya and Charlotte, whispered to each other when Amara approached. Maya wrinkled her nose at Amara's lunch box.

"What is that smell?" Maya said loudly. "It's like... weird spices or something."

Amara looked down at her jollof rice, her grandmother's recipe. The tomatoes and peppers gleamed red in the fluorescent light. Back home, everyone loved this dish. Here, it suddenly felt wrong.

"It's just rice," Amara mumbled, closing the lid.

"Why can't you eat normal food?" Charlotte added, giggling.

Sophie said nothing, but she didn't meet Amara's eyes either.

For two weeks, Amara brought plain sandwiches to school. She spoke less in class, even though she knew the answers. She tried to make herself smaller, quieter, less different.

Then came the International Day assembly. Each student could share something from their culture. Amara planned to skip it, but her English teacher, Mrs. Patterson, had other ideas.

"You have stories worth telling, Amara," Mrs. Patterson said gently. "Don't hide your light."

On the morning of the assembly, Amara's hands shook as she walked to the front. She had brought her grandmother's wrapper cloth, patterned with bold oranges and blues. She had also brought jollof rice for everyone to taste.

"In Nigeria, we say that food is love made visible," Amara began, her voice barely above a whisper. Then she remembered her grandmother's laugh, her mother's proud face at the station, and Mrs. Patterson's words. She lifted her chin and spoke louder. "This dish is called jollof rice. Every family has their own special way of making it. This is my grandmother's recipe, and she taught my mother, who taught me."

She talked about Lagos, about her grandmother's kitchen filled with laughter, about the colours and music of home. As she spoke, something shifted. Students leaned forward, listening. When she offered samples of the rice, hands shot up eagerly.

"This is amazing!" someone called out.

After the assembly, Maya approached hesitantly. "I'm sorry about what I said before. That was really mean. Your lunch looked different, and I... I didn't understand. But different isn't bad."

Amara considered the apology. Maya looked genuinely sorry, her cheeks pink with embarrassment.

"Thank you," Amara said finally. "Maybe next time, you could ask about things instead of judging them."

Maya nodded seriously. "I will. I promise."

That afternoon, Amara opened her lunch box without hesitation. The jollof rice smelled like home, like love, like courage. And this time, when others looked her way, she smiled back.

Passage:
Why Every Student Deserves a Musical Education
When school budgets are tight, music programmes are often the first to face the axe. This is a grave mistake that threatens the future of our children and our society.

Music education is not a luxury—it is a necessity. Research from universities worldwide consistently demonstrates that students who participate in music programmes perform better academically across all subjects. A landmark study involving over 100,000 students found that those who learnt musical instruments scored an average of 20% higher in mathematics and science tests. Why? Because music trains the brain to recognise patterns, maintain focus, and solve complex problems. These are precisely the skills that employers desperately seek in today's workforce.

Yet some administrators argue that music is merely entertainment, a pleasant distraction from 'real' learning. How short-sighted can we be? Would we dismiss mathematics because not every student becomes an engineer? Of course not. Music develops critical thinking, discipline, and creativity—qualities essential for success in any career path.

Consider the economic argument. Critics claim music programmes cost too much, but what price do we place on a child's potential? The instruments, teachers, and facilities required for music education cost less than many sports programmes, yet music receives a fraction of the funding. Schools routinely spend thousands on football equipment, but baulk at purchasing violins or hiring qualified music teachers. This reveals a troubling bias in our priorities.

Moreover, music education promotes mental health and emotional wellbeing. In an era where teenage anxiety and depression have reached alarming levels, music provides a vital outlet for self-expression and stress relief. Students who play instruments or sing in choirs report higher levels of confidence and stronger social connections. They learn teamwork through orchestras and bands, discovering how individual contributions create something greater than themselves.

The cultural benefits cannot be ignored either. Music connects us to our heritage and helps us understand diverse cultures. When students learn folk songs from different countries or explore various musical traditions, they develop empathy and global awareness. In our increasingly interconnected world, these qualities are invaluable.

Some argue that students can learn music outside school through private lessons. But this creates inequality. Only wealthy families can afford private tuition, meaning that talented children from disadvantaged backgrounds miss opportunities to develop their gifts. School music programmes level the playing field, ensuring every child—regardless of family income—can access this transformative education.

The evidence is overwhelming and the moral case is clear. We must prioritise music education in our schools, not as an optional extra, but as a fundamental component of every student's development. Our children deserve nothing less than a complete education—one that nurtures their minds, hearts, and souls. Will we give it to them?

What can be inferred about schools' spending priorities from the passage?

A) Schools spend equally on all extracurricular activities
B) Schools value sports programmes more than music programmes
C) Schools have unlimited budgets for student activities
D) Schools refuse to fund any music education

Passage: Why Every School Needs a Vegetable Garden

Walk into any school cafeteria today and you'll see something alarming: bins overflowing with uneaten vegetables whilst students line up for chips and sugary snacks. Is it any wonder that childhood obesity rates have tripled in the past thirty years? The solution is simpler than you might think, and it's growing right beneath our feet.

Every school in Australia should be required to maintain a vegetable garden where students grow, harvest and eat their own produce. This isn't just about healthy eating—it's about transforming how young people understand food, nature and responsibility.

Consider the evidence. Schools with vegetable gardens report that students eat 40% more vegetables than their peers. Why? Because children who grow tomatoes, carrots and lettuce develop a genuine connection to these foods. They've watched seeds sprout, pulled weeds in the hot sun, and felt the satisfaction of harvesting something they've nurtured. These vegetables aren't mysterious items from a supermarket shelf—they're the result of their own hard work.

But the benefits extend far beyond nutrition. School gardens are outdoor classrooms where mathematics, science and environmental studies come alive. Students measure garden beds, calculate growing seasons, observe insects and decomposition, and understand ecosystems firsthand. They learn that food doesn't magically appear in shops; it requires soil, water, time and care. This knowledge is disappearing from our society, and we're paying the price.

Critics argue that schools lack the space, time and money for gardens. These objections crumble under scrutiny. Even small courtyards can accommodate raised beds or vertical gardens. Garden maintenance requires just thirty minutes weekly, easily incorporated into existing science or health lessons. As for cost, initial setup expenses are minimal—soil, seeds and basic tools—whilst ongoing costs are virtually zero. Compare this to expensive sports equipment that schools replace annually.

Some educators worry about curriculum pressure, claiming there's no time for gardening when students must prepare for tests. This thinking is precisely backwards. Gardens don't distract from learning; they enhance it. Students retain information better when they're engaged in hands-on activities. A child who's planted beans understands plant biology far better than one who's merely read about it.

The question isn't whether we can afford school gardens. It's whether we can afford not to have them. With childhood diabetes rates soaring and environmental awareness more critical than ever, school gardens offer a practical, affordable solution. They teach students to value fresh food, understand where it comes from, and develop skills they'll use for life.

Isn't it time we gave every child the chance to get their hands dirty and discover that vegetables can actually be exciting? The evidence is clear, the benefits are numerous, and the cost is negligible. School gardens aren't a luxury—they're a necessity for the health and education of our children.

Why do students in schools with vegetable gardens eat more vegetables than other students?
A) Because vegetables from gardens taste better than store-bought ones
B) Because they feel personally connected to the food they have grown
C) Because their teachers force them to eat what they grow
D) Because garden vegetables are healthier than supermarket vegetables

Schools across Australia are abandoning cursive handwriting lessons in favour of keyboard skills, but this trend threatens vital cognitive development. Passage:
The Discovery in the Attic

When Liam's family moved into their grandmother's old house, he was assigned the smallest bedroom at the top of the stairs. While exploring one afternoon, he noticed a narrow door hidden behind a bookshelf. Behind it lay a cramped attic filled with boxes covered in decades of dust.

As Liam rummaged through old photographs and yellowed letters, he discovered a leather journal. The pages were brittle, but the handwriting was clear. It belonged to his great-grandfather, a soldier who had served in World War II. The entries described not battles or heroism, but small moments: sharing chocolate with local children, teaching a young French boy to play chess, planting a garden behind the military camp.

"What are you reading?" his grandmother asked, appearing in the doorway.

"Great-grandpa's journal," Liam replied. "I thought soldiers' diaries would be about fighting, but this is different."

His grandmother smiled softly. "Your great-grandfather believed that even in the darkest times, kindness was the most important thing. He said those small acts of humanity kept him sane when everything else was chaos."

Liam spent the rest of the summer reading the journal. He learned more about his great-grandfather from those simple stories than he ever had from history books. The man who emerged from the pages wasn't a war hero in the traditional sense, but someone who had fought to remain human in inhumane circumstances.

By summer's end, Liam had digitized the entire journal to preserve it. He realized that history wasn't just about major events, but also about the quiet choices ordinary people made every day.

B) Small acts of kindness and humanity can be significant even in difficult times
C) Soldiers in World War II kept detailed journals
D) Old houses often contain hidden treasures
E) Liam learns to appreciate his family's history

Passage:
Title: The Ancient Incan Road System: Connecting an Empire
High in the mountains of South America, an incredible network of roads once connected a vast empire. The Inca civilisation built over 40,000 kilometres of roads across some of the world's most challenging terrain. This remarkable achievement linked their empire from modern-day Colombia to Chile, crossing deserts, mountains, and rainforests.

The Incan road system, called Qhapaq Ñan, was constructed without the use of wheels or iron tools. Workers used stone hammers and bronze chisels to carve paths through solid rock. In flat areas, roads were up to 15 metres wide. In mountainous regions, they became narrow stone staircases climbing steep slopes. Some sections even included rope bridges that swung across deep valleys.

These roads served many important purposes for the empire. Messengers called chasquis ran along the routes, carrying news and information between cities. They could travel up to 240 kilometres in a single day by passing messages to the next runner at relay stations. The roads also allowed armies to move quickly across the empire, helping the Inca maintain control over distant regions. Additionally, traders used the routes to transport goods like food, textiles, and precious metals.

The Inca built their roads with careful planning and impressive engineering skills. In swampy areas, workers created raised causeways to keep the road surface dry. Through deserts, they placed marker posts to guide travellers when sand covered the path. In the mountains, they constructed drainage systems to prevent rainwater from washing away the roads. Stone retaining walls supported roads built on steep hillsides.

Rest houses called tambos were positioned every 20 to 30 kilometres along major routes. These buildings provided shelter, food, and supplies for official travellers. Storage facilities at tambos held emergency food reserves for times of famine. Some tambos were simple structures, whilst others were elaborate complexes with multiple buildings.

The road system reflected the Inca's strong central government and organised society. Only people on official business received permission to use the main roads. Common citizens needed special approval to travel. This control helped the empire manage resources and maintain security.

Title: Two Ways to Build an Igloo
The Inuit people of the Arctic developed two main methods for constructing igloos. The spiral method involves cutting blocks of hard-packed snow and arranging them in a continuous upward spiral, with each block leaning slightly inward. This creates a strong dome without needing support beams. The layer method stacks blocks in horizontal rings, one layer at a time, gradually narrowing toward the top. While the spiral method is faster and creates a smoother interior, the layer method is easier for beginners to learn and allows builders to check stability after each ring. Both techniques produce warm, wind-resistant shelters that can last an entire winter.

Title: How Poets Use Enjambment

Enjambment is a technique where a sentence or phrase runs from one line of poetry into the next without a pause or punctuation mark at the line break. This creates a flowing effect that can mirror movement or emotion. For example, a poem about a river might use enjambment to make the words spill down the page like water. Poets also use it to surprise readers by placing an unexpected word at the start of the next line. When a line ends with punctuation instead, this is called end-stopping. Enjambment keeps readers moving forward through the poem, building momentum and energy.

Title: How Glassblowers Shape Molten Sand

Glassblowing transforms ordinary sand into extraordinary art through intense heat and skilled manipulation. When silica sand reaches temperatures above 1400°C, it melts into a glowing, honey-like liquid that can be shaped before cooling. The process demands precise timing—glass that's too hot collapses, whilst glass that's too cool becomes rigid and cracks. Ancient Romans developed this technique over two thousand years ago, revolutionising glass production by making it faster and more versatile than previous mould-casting methods. Passage:
Title: How Lightning Rods Protect Buildings
Benjamin Franklin's ingenious invention, the lightning rod, operates through a sophisticated sequence of electrical principles. Initially, the pointed metal rod, mounted prominently atop a structure, creates an intensified electric field that attracts lightning strikes preferentially. Subsequently, when lightning connects with the rod, the electrical current is channelled safely through a thick conducting cable—typically copper or aluminium—that descends along the building's exterior. Finally, this immense electrical energy is dispersed harmlessly into the ground via a buried metal plate or rod system. This elegant three-stage process—attraction, conduction, and dissipation—has protected countless buildings from catastrophic fire and structural damage since the eighteenth century, exemplifying how understanding natural phenomena enables humanity to mitigate environmental hazards.

Title: Rival Theories on Dinosaur Extinction
Scientists have long debated what caused the dinosaurs to vanish 66 million years ago. The asteroid impact theory suggests a massive space rock struck Earth near modern-day Mexico, throwing dust into the atmosphere and blocking sunlight for years. This would have devastated plant life and collapsed food chains. However, the volcanic activity theory points to enormous eruptions in India that released toxic gases and altered the climate over thousands of years. Some researchers now believe both events occurred simultaneously, creating a catastrophic combination that even the mighty dinosaurs could not survive.

E) The asteroid theory is proven while the volcanic theory is speculation

Schools must reinstate dedicated handwriting lessons before we lose this essential skill forever. While keyboards dominate our digital world, research consistently shows that students who write by hand retain information more effectively and develop superior fine motor skills. Passage:
Title: Two Strategies for Learning a New Language
Language learners often debate whether immersion or structured study produces better results. Immersion advocates argue that surrounding yourself with native speakers forces rapid adaptation and natural pronunciation. Living in a country where the target language is spoken daily creates countless opportunities for authentic practice. However, structured study supporters emphasise the importance of grammar foundations and systematic vocabulary building. They believe that understanding grammatical rules prevents fossilised errors and provides a framework for self-correction. Many successful polyglots suggest combining both approaches: establishing core grammar through formal lessons whilst simultaneously exposing yourself to native content through films, podcasts, and conversation partners. This hybrid method addresses both accuracy and fluency simultaneously.

The primary difference between immersion and structured study approaches is:
A) The speed of learning versus the cost of learning
B) Natural pronunciation versus systematic vocabulary building
C) Authentic practice opportunities versus grammatical foundations
D) The number of languages learned versus the quality of learning
E) Native speakers versus textbook authors

Title: How Forensic Entomologists Solve Crimes

Forensic entomologists employ insects as biological clocks to determine time of death, a technique that has revolutionised criminal investigations. When investigators discover a body, blowflies are often the first arrivals, laying eggs within minutes of death. By identifying the species present and measuring the developmental stage of larvae, scientists can calculate how long decomposition has been occurring. This method proves particularly valuable when bodies remain undiscovered for extended periods, as traditional pathology becomes unreliable after seventy-two hours. Temperature fluctuations, geographic location, and seasonal variations all influence insect activity, requiring entomologists to meticulously account for environmental factors. Despite its macabre nature, this scientific discipline has exonerated innocent suspects and secured convictions in numerous cold cases worldwide.

Passage:
Title: Contrasting Approaches to Urban Beekeeping
Urban beekeeping has flourished through two divergent methodologies. Traditional apiarists favour the Langstroth hive system, which employs removable frames that facilitate systematic honey extraction and colony inspection. This interventionist approach maximises yield but requires frequent disturbance of the bees' natural rhythms. Conversely, proponents of the Top Bar hive embrace a minimalist philosophy, prioritising bee welfare over productivity. Their horizontal design mimics hollow logs, allowing colonies to construct comb organically with minimal human interference. Whilst Langstroth advocates emphasise economic viability and disease management through regular monitoring, Top Bar enthusiasts argue their method produces healthier, more resilient bee populations adapted to urban microclimates, despite yielding considerably less honey.

The fundamental difference between Langstroth and Top Bar beekeeping philosophies centres on:
A) The colour and design of the hive structures
B) Whether hives should be placed in urban or rural locations
C) The balance between human intervention and bee autonomy
D) The species of bees that can survive in each system
E) The temperature requirements for successful honey production

Passage:
Title: Two Paths to the Summit
Mount Kilimanjaro, Africa's highest peak, can be conquered via several routes, but the Marangu and Machame trails offer strikingly different experiences. The Marangu route, nicknamed the 'Coca-Cola route', features sleeping huts with bunk beds and follows a gentler, more gradual ascent over five to six days. Climbers appreciate its relative comfort and shorter duration. In contrast, the Machame route—known as the 'Whiskey route'—demands camping in tents and tackles steeper terrain across six to seven days. Though more physically challenging, it provides superior acclimatisation opportunities and more dramatic scenery, resulting in higher summit success rates despite the increased difficulty.

The main advantage of the Machame route over the Marangu route is:
A) It takes less time to complete
B) It offers more comfortable sleeping arrangements
C) It has a gentler, more gradual ascent
D) It provides better acclimatisation and higher success rates
E) It is less physically demanding
